CSCI2520
Data Structures & Applications
Graphs
Graph in daily life
What is a Graph?
A graph consists of some vertices (a.k.a. nodes) and some edges (a.k.a. arcs).

What is a Graph?
A graph consists of some vertices and some edges.
A graph with 5 vertices and 2 edges.
Vertices:
v1, v2, v3, v4, v5
Edges:
e1 = (v2, v3)
e2 = (v2, v4)
What is a Graph?
A graph consists of some vertices and some edges.
A graph with 5 vertices and 0 edges.
Vertices:
v1, v2, v3, v4, v5
Edges:
---------
What is a Graph?
A graph consists of some vertices and some edges.
A graph with 4 vertices and 6 edges.
Vertices:
v1, v2, v3, v4
Edges:
e1 = (v1, v2)
e2 = (v1, v3)
e3 = (v3, v4)
e4 = (v2, v4)
e5 = (v1, v4)
e6 = (v2, v3)
Graph and Tree
A tree is also a graph (but not vice versa).
A graph with 11 vertices and 10 edges.
Directed Graph
Edges in a graph can be directed. A graph with directed edges is a directed graph (digraph).
Vertices:
v1, v2, v3, v4, v5
Edges:
e1 = (v1, v2)
e2 = (v2, v3)
e3 = (v4, v2)
e4 = (v3, v5)
e5 = (v4, v5)
e6 = (v5, v4)
A directed graph with 5 vertices and 6 directed edges.

Directed and Undirected Graphs
Directed graph
Undirected graph
These two graphs are different.
Weighted Graph
Edges in a graph can have weights. A graph with weighted edges is a weighted graph.
Vertices:
v1, v2, v3, v4, v5
Edges:
(v1, v2), weight = 3
(v2, v3), weight = 9
(v2, v4), weight = 12
(v3, v5), weight = 5
(v4, v5), weight = 8
(Un)directed (Un)weighted Graph
Directed weighted graph
Directed unweighted graph
Undirected weighted graph
Undirected unweighted graph
All four graphs are different.
Graph Terminologies

A simple path is a path with no duplicating vertices.
E.g., the path v1, v2, v3, v5 is simple, while v5, v4, v5, v4, v2 is not simple.

Graph Terminologies
A cycle: v2, v3, v5, v4, v2

A simple cycle is a cycle with no duplicating vertices except the first and last vertex.
An acyclic graph is a graph with no cycles.
A directed acyclic graph is sometimes called a DAG.

In an undirected graph, two vertices u and v are connected if there is a path from u to v.
An undirected graph is connected if all pairs of vertices are connected.

Graph and Tree
A tree is a connected acyclic undirected graph.
A graph with 11 vertices and 10 edges.
Graph Connectivity
A directed graph is weakly connected if replacing all directed edges with undirected edges produces a connected (undirected) graph.

Graph Connectivity
A directed graph is strongly connected if for every pair of vertices, u and v, it contains a directed path from u to v.
A strongly connected graph
Not a strongly connected graph

Note that a strongly connected graph is also a weakly connected graph, but not vice versa.
Degree of Graph
In a directed graph,
the out-degree of a vertex v is the number of edges whose head is v.
the in-degree of a vertex v is the number of edges whose tail is v.

Adjacency List: Weighted Graph
In a weighted graph, the elements of list L[i] contains the vertices vj that are adjacent to vi as well as the weights weight(vi, vj).

Graph Traversals
Traversing a graph means visiting each vertex of the graph exactly once.
Two common graph traversal algorithms
Depth first search (DFS)
Breadth first search (BFS)
Depth First Search
Order of visit: v0
Depth First Search
Order of visit: v0, v1
Depth First Search
Order of visit: v0, v1, v3
Depth First Search
Order of visit: v0, v1, v3, v7
Depth First Search

Spanning Tree
A spanning tree (ST) of an undirected graph is a tree which contains all vertices and some edges of the graph.
Graph G
A spanning tree of G
Another spanning tree of G
Spanning Tree and Connectivity
An undirected graph has a spanning tree if and only if the graph is connected.
A graph that has no spanning trees
Spanning Tree
For a graph with n vertices, its spanning tree always has exactly n - 1 edges.
Graph G
A spanning tree of G
Another spanning tree of G
Minimum Spanning Tree
A minimum spanning tree (MST) of an undirected weighted graph is a spanning tree whose sum of all weights is minimum.
A spanning tree of G
(total weight = 3+9+12+5 = 29)
A MST of G
(total weight = 3+9+5+8 = 25)
Graph G
MST Algorithms

[Content_Types].xmlPK
_rels/.relsPK
drs/downrev.xmlPK
Spans all vertices. Done!
Total tree weight = 39
Increasingly sort the edges by weights.
For each edge e in sorted order
If e does not form a cycle with the already picked edges, then
Pick e. (Done when n - 1 edges are picked.)
Else
Discard e.
If fewer than n - 1 edges are picked, then
Graph is disconnected. No MST exists.
Start from any one vertex, say, v0.
(Consider v0 as a tree with one node.)

[Content_Types].xmlPK
_rels/.relsPK
drs/downrev.xmlPK
Spans all vertices. Done!
Total tree weight = 39
Initialize the current tree T to have any one vertex.
While T has fewer than n - 1 edges
Pick the edge around T whose weight is the smallest and does not form a cycle.
(When no such edge is found, graph is disconnected and no MST exists.)

shortest path
v0 to v1:
v0, v1
v0 to v2:
v0, v2
v0 to v3:

v0, v2, v4
v0 to v5:
v0, v2, v4, v3, v5

[Content_Types].xmlPK
_rels/.relsPK
drs/downrev.xmlPK
During the algorithm, a visited vertex vj means the shortest path from v0 (start) to vj has been found already.
Initialize the current best cost table.
While not all vertices are visited
Pick the unvisited vertex v whose current best cost is minimum.
For any other vertex u that can be reached cheaper via v
Update the current best cost and path of u.
